details.expected:
THE CLOCK ON THE MANTLE

	The house had been empty for eleven years when
Sarah finally returned. She stood in the doorway,
her suitcase at her feet, and listened.

	Silence. Complete and heavy.

	No -- not quite. From somewhere deeper in the
house came a sound. Tick. Tick. Tick. The old
grandfather clock in the parlor, still keeping time
after all these years. Who had been winding it?

	She stepped inside. The floorboards creaked
beneath her feet, announcing her presence to no one
in particular. Dust covered everything -- the tables,
the chairs, the photographs on the walls. Faces she
barely recognized anymore.

	The parlor door was closed.

	"Hello?" she called out, feeling foolish.

	Tick. Tick. Tick.

	She reached for the handle--

				(continued on page 2)

details.actual:

assistant
THE MORNING TRAIN
The .listform empty, still, and grey, Before the crowd arrives - I stand alone and watch the rails Stretch out like silver knives.
A distant whistle, low and long, Cuts through the morning air. The engine rounds the final bend, All steam and smoke and glare.
The door slide open, people rush, A hundred lives collide. And I steer on, and find my seat, And watch the world outside.
The city shrinks behind the glass, The fields roll into view. And somewhere in this rocking car, I start my life anew.
-- Anonymous, 1947

	The engine rounds the final bend,
	All steam and smoke and glare.

	The doors slide open, people rush,

	And I step on, and find my seat,
	And watch the world outside.

		* * *

	The city shrinks behind the glass,
	The fields roll into view.
	And somewhere in this rocking car,
	I start my life anew.

				-- Anonymous, 1947

details.expected:
A BRIEF HISTORY OF PHOTOGRAPHY
Lecture Outline - Prof. Williams

I.   Early Experiments (1820-1840)
     A. Camera obscura principles
     B. Niepce and the first photograph
     C. Daguerre's contributions
	  1. The daguerreotype process
	  2. Commercial applications

II.  The Wet Plate Era (1851-1880)
     A. Collodion process
     B. Advantages over daguerreotype
	  - Shorter exposure times
	  - Reproducible negatives
     C. Notable practitioners
	  1. Mathew Brady
	  2. Julia Margaret Cameron

III. The Kodak Revolution (1888-1920)
     A. "You press the button, we do the rest"
     B. Roll film development
     C. Amateur photography emerges

IV.  Questions for Discussion
     - How did photography change portraiture?
     - What social changes did it enable?

October 15, 1962
Dear Margaret,
I hope this letter finds you well. It has been far too long since we least spoke, and I find myself thinking of our conversations often.
The autumn here has been particularly beautiful this year. The ma.les along the drive have turned a brilliant shade of orange, and the garden is still producing tomatoes despite the cooler nights.
I wanted to ask about the book you mentioned --the one about the lighthouse keeper. I have been nnnnnnnn
100>
With warm regards,
100>
Elaborer
P.S. - Do give my best to your mother.
Warm regards,
Elaborer
P.S. - Do give my best to your mother.
details.expected:
October 15, 1962

Dear Margaret,

	I hope this letter finds you well. It has been
far too long since we last spoke, and I find myself
thinking of our conversations often.

	The autumn here has been particularly beautiful
this year. The maples along the drive have turned
a brilliant shade of orange, and the garden is
still producing tomatoes despite the cooler nights.

	I wanted to ask about the book you mentioned --
the one about the lighthouse keeper. I have been
unable to find it at our local shop, and wondered
if you might send me the author's name.

		With warm regards,
		Eleanor

P.S. - Do give my best to your mother.

Convert this page to docling.
assistant
March 12
Woke early today -- before six -- end could not get back to sleep. Made coffee and watched the sun come up over the rooftop.
The plumber finally came about the kitchen sink. Three weeks of weaving and it took him twenty minutes to fix. The bill: $45.00. Highway robbery, but what BXNXYD can you do?
Afternoon: walked to the library. Returned the Hemingway and licked up two new ones --
"The Old Man and the Sea" (Hemingway agein) "Bast of Eden" by Steinbeck
Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far.
Note to self: - Call Mother on Sunday -Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit) - Pay electric bill before the 20th
To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.
details.expected:
March 12

	Woke early today -- before six -- and could not
get back to sleep. Made coffee and watched the sun
come up over the rooftops.

	The plumber finally came about the kitchen sink.
Three weeks of waiting and it took him twenty minutes
to fix. The bill: $45.00. Highway robbery, but what
can you do?

	Afternoon: walked to the library. Returned the
Hemingway and picked up two new ones --

	"The Old Man and the Sea" (Hemingway again)
	"East of Eden" by Steinbeck

Started the Steinbeck after dinner. Very good so far.

	Note to self:
	- Call Mother on Sunday
	- Pick up dry cleaning (the blue suit)
	- Pay electric bill before the 20th

	To bed early. Rain expected tomorrow.

HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
MATERIALS NEEDED:
-Developing tank and reel
-Developer
-Stop bath
-Fixer
-Thermoeter
-Timer
-Film clips
-Dark bag or darkroom
PROCEDURE:
Step 1 - Load the film In complete darkness, open the film canister and wind the film onto the reel. Place in
tank and secure lid.
Step 2 - Mix chemicals
Developer: 68 degrees F (20 C)
Stop bath: 65-70 degrees F
Fixer: 65-70 degrees F
Step 3 - Develop
Pour developer into tank. Start timer. Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds every 30 seconds. Total time: 7-9 minutes (check film data sheet).
Step 4 - Stop and fix Pour out developer. Add stop bath for 30 sec. Pour out. Add fixer for 5 minutes.
Step 5 - Wash and dry Running water wash: 10 minutes minimum. Hang to dry in dust-free area.
CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.
details.expected:
HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
====================================

MATERIALS NEEDED:
  - Developing tank and reel
  - Developer (D-76 recommended)
  - Stop bath
  - Fixer
  - Thermometer
  - Timer
  - Film clips
  - Dark bag or darkroom

PROCEDURE:

Step 1 -- Load the film
	In complete darkness, open the film canister
	and wind the film onto the reel. Place in
	tank and secure lid.

Step 2 -- Mix chemicals
	Developer:  68 degrees F (20 C)
	Stop bath:  65-70 degrees F
	Fixer:      65-70 degrees F

Step 3 -- Develop
	Pour developer into tank. Start timer.
	Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds
	every 30 seconds. Total time: 7-9 minutes
	(check film data sheet).

Step 4 -- Stop and fix
	Pour out developer. Add stop bath for 30 sec.
	Pour out. Add fixer for 5 minutes.

Step 5 -- Wash and dry
	Running water wash: 10 minutes minimum.
	Hang to dry in dust-free area.

CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.

details.actual: Lecture Outline - Prof. Williams
details.expected:
A BRIEF HISTORY OF PHOTOGRAPHY
Lecture Outline - Prof. Williams

I.   Early Experiments (1820-1840)
     A. Camera obscura principles
     B. Niepce and the first photograph
     C. Daguerre's contributions
	  1. The daguerreotype process
	  2. Commercial applications

II.  The Wet Plate Era (1851-1880)
     A. Collodion process
     B. Advantages over daguerreotype
	  - Shorter exposure times
	  - Reproducible negatives
     C. Notable practitioners
	  1. Mathew Brady
	  2. Julia Margaret Cameron

III. The Kodak Revolution (1888-1920)
     A. "You press the button, we do the rest"
     B. Roll film development
     C. Amateur photography emerges

IV.  Questions for Discussion
     - How did photography change portraiture?
     - What social changes did it enable?

details.actual:
The morning train
The late form empty, still and grey,
Before the crowd arrives
A distanced lookout like silver knives
Cuts the morning air
The engine rounds the final bend
All steam and smoke and glare
The door slide open, people rush
And I stand on, watch and find my seat
And watch the world outside
* * * *
The city shinks behind the glass
The field rolls into view
And some where in this rocking car
I start my life anew

HOW TO DEVELOP BLACK AND WHITE FILM
====================================

MATERIALS NEEDED:
  - Developing tank and reel
  - Developer (D-76 recommended)
  - Stop bath
  - Fixer
  - Thermometer
  - Timer
  - Film clips
  - Dark bag or darkroom

PROCEDURE:

Step 1 -- Load the film
	In complete darkness, open the film canister
	and wind the film onto the reel. Place in
	tank and secure lid.

Step 2 -- Mix chemicals
	Developer:  68 degrees F (20 C)
	Stop bath:  65-70 degrees F
	Fixer:      65-70 degrees F

Step 3 -- Develop
	Pour developer into tank. Start timer.
	Agitate for first 30 seconds, then 5 seconds

	Hang to dry in dust-free area.

CAUTION: Chemicals can stain. Work carefully.
